content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 20 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic) | **Chapter:** Deuteronomy 20 (20 verses) | **LC:** pp. 217–218 | **Vaticanus:** pp. 155–156

---

**1** When you go out to battle against your enemies and see horses and chariots and an army larger than yours, you shall not be afraid of them, for the LORD your God is with you, who brought you up from the land of Egypt. **2** And when you draw near to the battle, the priest shall approach and speak to the people **3** and shall say to them: "Hear, O Israel! Today you are drawing near to battle against your enemies. Let not your heart faint; do not be afraid, or tremble, or be in dread of them, **4** for the LORD your God is he who goes with you to fight for you against your enemies, to give you victory."

**5** Then the officers shall speak to the people, saying: "Is there any man who has built a new house and has not dedicated it? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man dedicate it. **6** Is there any man who has planted a vineyard and has not yet enjoyed its fruit? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man enjoy it. **7** Is there any man who has betrothed a woman and has not married her? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man marry her." **8** And the officers shall speak further to the people and say: "Is there any man who is fearful and fainthearted? Let him go back to his house, lest the heart of his brothers faint like his heart." **9** And when the officers have finished speaking to the people, they shall appoint commanders of armies at the head of the people.

**10** When you draw near to a city to fight against it, you shall offer it terms of peace. **11** And if it responds to you peaceably and opens to you, then all the people found in it shall do forced labor for you and shall serve you. **12** But if it makes no peace with you and makes war against you, then you shall besiege it. **13** And when the LORD your God gives it into your hand, you shall strike all its males with the edge of the sword. **14** But the women and the little ones, the livestock, and everything in the city—all its spoil—you shall take as plunder for yourself. And you shall enjoy the spoil of your enemies that the LORD your God has given to you. **15** Thus you shall do to all the cities that are very far from you, which are not cities of these nations.

**16** But in the cities of these peoples that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance, you shall save alive nothing that breathes, **17** but you shall devote them to destruction—the Hittites and the Amorites, the Canaanites and the Perizzites, the Hivites and the Jebusites—as the LORD your God has commanded you, **18** that they may not teach you to do according to all their abominable practices that they have done for their gods, and so you would sin against the LORD your God.

**19** When you besiege a city for a long time, fighting against it in order to capture it, you shall not destroy its trees by wielding an axe against them. You may eat from them, but you shall not cut them down. For is the tree of the field a man, that it should come against you in the siege? **20** Only the trees that you know are not food trees you may destroy and cut down, so that you may build siege works against the city that makes war with you, until it falls.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Chapter overview.** Deuteronomy 20 addresses three interlocking subjects: the war-priest's speech and military exemptions (vv. 1–9), the law of terms of surrender for distant cities versus the herem command for Canaanite cities (vv. 10–18), and the prohibition on cutting down fruit trees during a siege (vv. 19–20). The three agents agree closely on the core rendering.

**v.3 Four verbs of fear.** EMET renders all four Hebrew verbs distinctly: "grow faint / be afraid / panic / be terrified." LOGOS uses "faint / be afraid / tremble / be in dread." PNEUMA collapses to "lose heart or panic / be terrified or filled with dread." Consensus follows LOGOS's four-term rendering, preserving each verb while maintaining readability.

**v.4 "to give you victory" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "to save you" (EMET).** Hebrew *le-hoshi'a*. The root *yasha'* means "to save, deliver, give victory." "To save you" (EMET) is the literal-base rendering; "to give you victory" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) interprets the military context. Consensus adopts "to give you victory" as contextually apt and supported by LOGOS/PNEUMA.

**v.6 "enjoyed its fruit" vs. "made common use of it."** EMET: "make common use of it" (rendering *chilelo* technically). LOGOS/PNEUMA: "enjoyed its fruit." The Hebrew *chilel* (Pi'el) is the technical term for "releasing from prohibition" (Lev 19:23–25). "Enjoyed its fruit" is what this technically means in practice. Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA as the more accessible rendering without obscuring the legal concept (explained in notes).

**v.9 "commanders of armies" vs. "military commanders."** EMET/LOGOS: "commanders of armies" (*sarey tseva'ot*). PNEUMA: "military commanders." Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the construct chain.

**v.16 "nothing that breathes" vs. "any soul."** EMET: "any soul" (*kol neshama*). LOGOS/PNEUMA: "nothing that breathes." Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "nothing that breathes" captures both the totality and the meaning of *neshama* (breath/life).

**v.17 "devote them to destruction" vs. "utterly devote them to destruction."** EMET: "utterly devote them to destruction" (cognate construction *charema tachrimem*). LOGOS: "devote them to destruction." Consensus follows LOGOS without "utterly" as the force is already carried by "destruction." The absolute devotion is clear in context.

**v.19 Rhetorical question.** All three preserve the question form "For is the tree of the field a man?" — consistent with MT and Vaticanus. PNEUMA: "Is the tree a person?" — abbreviated. Consensus retains the full form (EMET/LOGOS).

**Vaticanus note.** Vaticanus pp. 155–156 matches MT throughout, including the herem formula in v. 17 (*anathemati anathematieseis autous*). No additions or omissions.

---

*Arbiter: SOPHIA*
